title: Practice Set 4 — Selecting and Filtering Data
description: Apply the core data shaping operations: selecting columns with select() and alias(), filtering rows with compound conditions, handling null values, removing duplicates, and chaining transformations into a complete Bronze-to-Silver pipeline.

datasets.description: NordGrid daily meter readings with extra Bronze-layer columns. 121 rows including 8 nulls, 5 negatives, 2 zeros, and 6 duplicate meter_id+date keys.

columns.name: region
columns.description: Operating region: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, or WEST

columns.name: ingestion_ts
columns.description: Timestamp when the row was ingested into Bronze

columns.name: source_file
columns.description: Name of the CSV batch file the row came from

characteristics.zeros: 2 rows have daily_kw of exactly 0.0 (valid readings)
characteristics.duplicates: 6 meter_id+date key pairs appear twice (overlapping ingestion windows)
characteristics.extraColumns: ingestion_ts, source_file, batch_id are Bronze metadata not needed in Silver

tasks.title: Shape NordGrid's Readings for Silver

description.context: NordGrid's Bronze meter readings table has seven columns, but the Silver schema requires only four: meter_id, energy_kw, reading_date, and region. The column names in the Bronze CSV do not match the Silver contract — daily_kw must become energy_kw and date must become reading_date. The three Bronze metadata columns (ingestion_ts, source_file, batch_id) must be excluded.
description.task: Read PS4_data_readings.csv (with header=True and inferSchema=True) and use a single select() call with alias() to produce a DataFrame with exactly four columns named meter_id, energy_kw, reading_date, and region — in that order. Store the result in a variable called silver_shaped.
description.validationHint: Your output should have exactly 4 columns in the correct order. The row count should remain 121 — select does not remove rows.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-silver-shaping') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PS4_data_readings.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Bronze columns: {df.columns}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Silver columns: {silver_shaped.columns}")
print(f"Row count: {silver_shaped.count()}")
silver_shaped.show(5)

assertions.description: Exactly 4 columns in the correct order
Row count unchanged (select does not filter rows)
energy_kw contains the same values as the original daily_kw (alias, not computed)

tasks.partialCredit: 4 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/4 credit.

tasks.title: Filter NordGrid's Northern High-Usage Meters

description.context: NordGrid's operations team wants to identify high-usage meters in the NORTH and EAST regions for a capacity planning review. They define high-usage as any meter with daily_kw above 1500. The report must exclude null readings and negative sensor errors, but should include readings of exactly 0.0 (meters that reported but measured no consumption are still relevant for capacity planning).
description.task: Starting from the full readings DataFrame, apply filters to produce a DataFrame containing only rows where: (1) daily_kw is not null, (2) daily_kw is greater than or equal to 0, (3) daily_kw is greater than 1500, and (4) region is either NORTH or EAST. Use the chained filter pattern with one condition per filter call. Store the result in a variable called high_usage.
description.validationHint: Apply the filters in the order listed. The null filter must come first to avoid null comparison issues. Use isin() for the region check.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-capacity-review') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PS4_data_readings.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Total rows: {df.count()}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"High-usage rows: {high_usage.count()}")
high_usage.show(5)

assertions.description: Only NORTH and EAST regions present

tasks.partialCredit: 5 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/5 credit.

tasks.title: Predict How Filters Handle Null Readings

description.context: A NordGrid engineer writes three different filter expressions on the same meter readings dataset (121 rows, 8 of which have null daily_kw). The engineer is confused about why the row counts differ between what appear to be equivalent filters.
description.task: Read each filter expression and predict how many rows it keeps. Pay careful attention to how null values behave in each comparison.
description.validationHint: Remember: when daily_kw is null, any comparison (>, <, ==, !=) evaluates to null, and filter() discards null results along with false results.

evidence.content:
df = spark.read.csv('PS4_data_readings.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
# df has 121 rows total
# 8 rows have null daily_kw
# 5 rows have negative daily_kw
# 2 rows have daily_kw == 0.0
# 106 rows have positive daily_kw

result_a = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
result_b = df.filter(col('daily_kw') >= 0)
result_c = df.filter(~(col('daily_kw') > 0))

fields.label: How many rows does result_a contain? (filter: daily_kw > 0)
fields.rationale: daily_kw > 0 keeps the 106 positive rows. Nulls are discarded (null > 0 → null → discarded). Negatives are discarded (negative > 0 → false). Zeros are discarded (0 > 0 → false).

fields.label: How many rows does result_b contain? (filter: daily_kw >= 0)
fields.rationale: daily_kw >= 0 keeps the 106 positive rows plus 2 zero-value rows (0 >= 0 → true). Nulls are discarded. Negatives are discarded.

fields.label: How many rows does result_c contain? (filter: NOT daily_kw > 0)
fields.rationale: ~(col('daily_kw') > 0) inverts the boolean. For positive values: NOT true → false → discarded. For negatives: NOT false → true → kept (5 rows). For zeros: NOT false → true → kept (2 rows). For nulls: NOT null → null → discarded. Total: 5 + 2 = 7. The trap: students might expect 121 - 106 = 15, but nulls are discarded by both the original and the negated filter.

fields.label: Why don't result_a and result_c add up to 121 (the total rows)?
options: Because filter() has a rounding error when counting rows
Because null rows are discarded by both filters — null is neither true nor false
Because zero-value rows are counted in both results
Because duplicate rows are removed automatically
fields.correctAnswer: Because null rows are discarded by both filters — null is neither true nor false
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 is implausible but sounds like a real bug. Option 3 would cause over-counting, not under-counting. Option 4 confuses filtering with deduplication. The key insight is SQL's three-valued logic: null comparisons produce null, and null is filtered out regardless of negation.

tasks.title: Deduplicate NordGrid's Overlapping Batches

description.context: NordGrid's Bronze ingestion pipeline ran twice within an overlapping window, producing duplicate readings for some meters. The same meter's reading for the same date appears in two different batch files. These duplicates will inflate regional totals if they reach Silver. The team needs to keep exactly one reading per meter per date combination.
description.task: Read PS4_data_readings.csv and remove duplicate rows based on the meter_id and date columns using dropDuplicates(). The daily_kw values in duplicate rows are identical, so keeping an arbitrary row is safe. Then verify the duplicates are gone. Store the deduplicated result in a variable called deduped.
description.validationHint: The dataset has 121 rows with 6 duplicate meter_id+date key pairs. After deduplication, you should have 115 rows.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-dedup') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PS4_data_readings.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Before dedup: {df.count()} rows")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"After dedup: {deduped.count()} rows")
print(f"Columns: {deduped.columns}")

assertions.description: All columns preserved (dropDuplicates does not remove columns)

tasks.partialCredit: 4 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/4 credit.

description.context: NordGrid's senior engineer is reviewing a junior engineer's Bronze-to-Silver pipeline code. The junior engineer made several transformation choices that the senior needs to evaluate. For each choice, identify whether it is correct, incorrect, or a matter of style preference.
description.task: Read each code snippet and determine what happens when it runs. Focus on the behavior of select vs drop, filter vs where, and the effect of chaining on performance.
description.validationHint: Remember that filter() and where() are aliases, chained filters are fused by Catalyst, and drop() silently ignores missing columns.

fields.label: Code: df.drop('nonexistent_column') — what happens?
options: Spark raises an error because the column does not exist
Spark silently returns the DataFrame unchanged — no error
Spark removes all columns and returns an empty DataFrame
Spark removes the first column as a fallback
fields.correctAnswer: Spark silently returns the DataFrame unchanged — no error
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 is the behavior of select() with a missing column, not drop(). This distinction is a key concept from Lesson 3.

fields.label: Are df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0) and df.where(col('daily_kw') > 0) functionally different?
options: Yes — filter() is faster because it was optimized first
Yes — where() checks for nulls automatically while filter() does not
No — they are exact aliases with identical behavior and performance
No — they produce the same result but where() skips Catalyst optimization
fields.correctAnswer: No — they are exact aliases with identical behavior and performance
fields.distractorRationale: Options 1 and 4 suggest performance differences that don't exist. Option 2 invents a null-handling difference. The theory is explicit: same implementation, same behavior.

fields.label: A pipeline chains 5 filter() calls: df.filter(A).filter(B).filter(C).filter(D).filter(E). Does Spark scan the data 5 times?
options: Yes — each filter triggers a separate data scan
No — Spark fuses them into one compound filter and scans the data once when an action triggers
No — Spark only executes the last filter and ignores the first four
It depends on whether the filters use & or are chained separately
fields.correctAnswer: No — Spark fuses them into one compound filter and scans the data once when an action triggers
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 ignores lazy evaluation. Option 3 misunderstands plan building. Option 4 is a subtle trap — the theory explicitly states that chained filters and compound & filters produce the same plan.

fields.label: The engineer writes: df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0 & col('region') == 'NORTH'). What happens?
options: It correctly filters for positive readings in the NORTH region
It raises a TypeError because of operator precedence — parentheses are required around each condition
It filters for NORTH only and ignores the daily_kw condition
It filters for positive daily_kw only and ignores the region condition
fields.correctAnswer: It raises a TypeError because of operator precedence — parentheses are required around each condition
fields.distractorRationale: Options 3 and 4 describe partial execution that doesn't happen. Option 1 is what the engineer intended. The correct answer tests knowledge of the parentheses rule from Lesson 5.

tasks.title: Build NordGrid's Complete Bronze-to-Silver Pipeline

description.context: NordGrid's data engineering team needs a complete Bronze-to-Silver pipeline for the daily meter readings. The pipeline must handle every data quality issue present in the Bronze dataset: null readings, negative sensor errors, duplicate ingestion batches, and extra metadata columns. The Silver output must have exactly four columns named meter_id, energy_kw, reading_date, and region.
description.task: Write a chained transformation pipeline that: (1) reads PS4_data_readings.csv with header and inferSchema; (2) filters out null daily_kw values; (3) filters out negative daily_kw values but keeps zeros; (4) removes duplicate rows based on meter_id and date; (5) produces the final output with exactly four columns — meter_id, daily_kw aliased as energy_kw, date aliased as reading_date, and region. Use the parenthesized chaining style. Store the result in a variable called silver.
description.validationHint: Start with 121 rows. Remove 8 nulls (→ 113). Remove 5 negatives (→ 108). Remove 6 duplicates (→ 102). Final schema: 4 columns. The order of dedup vs filter may slightly change the count if duplicates have null/negative values — apply filters first, then dedup.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-bronze-to-silver') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '8') \
    .getOrCreate()

readings_path = 'PS4_data_readings.csv'

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
silver.printSchema()
print(f"Silver row count: {silver.count()}")
print(f"Silver columns: {silver.columns}")
silver.show(5)

assertions.description: Exactly 4 columns with correct Silver names
Correct final row count after all cleaning

tasks.partialCredit: 7 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/7 credit.

conceptsCombined: select() with alias() for column shaping and renaming (Lessons 1, 8)
Chained filter() calls for null and negative removal (Lessons 4-6)
dropDuplicates() on key columns for dedup (Lesson 9)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This task is the full Bronze-to-Silver pipeline that Module 4 builds toward. The student must combine column shaping, null handling, negative filtering with zero preservation, deduplication, and renaming in a single chained pipeline. The order matters: filtering before dedup is the correct pattern because it removes invalid rows before dedup considers key uniqueness.

selfCheck.foundationModuleException: Not applicable — Module 4 is Core Mechanics phase.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory examples use daily_kw > 0 and standard column names. Tasks require >= 0 (preserving zeros), aliasing to non-standard names (energy_kw, reading_date), and deduplication on key columns — same techniques, different parameters. Task 3 specifically tests the null behavior that the theory warns about but does not make students calculate.
selfCheck.datasetNotes: PS4_data_readings.csv has 121 rows with 7 columns (3 extra Bronze metadata columns). Key characteristics: 8 nulls, 5 negatives, 2 zeros, 6 duplicate meter_id+date pairs. The extra columns force students to use select/drop rather than working with a pre-shaped dataset. Duplicates have identical daily_kw values so dropDuplicates on key columns is safe.
